one_line_takeaway: CourtGuard swaps fine-tuned safety classifiers for a RAG-grounded Attacker/Defender/Judge debate over policy documents, letting a single architecture hit SOTA-competitive accuracy across 8 safety benchmarks while adapting zero-shot to new policies (90% accuracy on out-of-domain Wikipedia vandalism, matching a dedicated policy-following model) purely by swapping the retrieved corpus, at the cost of higher inference latency and dependence on backbone instruction-following.
key_finding: CourtGuard-GPT-OSS-20B (2 Iter) attains the best macro-average across the 8 benchmarks: 0.87 accuracy / 0.86 F1, versus LlamaGuard 4's 0.77/0.75 (paper states +10% accuracy improvement) and versus the best generic LLM-judge baselines (Reasoning Llama-70B and Multi-Agent Voting variants, all at 0.86 macro accuracy). On the Human-Verified Gold Standard suite, CourtGuard-GPT-OSS-20B (1 Iter) achieves the top Recall (0.957), F1 (0.904), F2 (0.935), and ROC AUC (0.924), while GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B still leads on raw Accuracy (0.919) -- the paper attributes this to that baseline's system prompt being purpose-built for the exact MLCommons taxonomy used as ground truth. On zero-shot out-of-domain transfer, swapping the RAG corpus to WP:VANDAL (no fine-tuning) yields 90% accuracy on Wikipedia vandalism detection, matching the specialized gpt-oss-safeguard-20b (90%) and beating the base gpt-oss-20b judge (86%). On a 33-case GDPR/InfoSoc legal-adaptation spot check, a human legal expert agreed with CourtGuard's reasoning traces and verdicts 93.9% of the time. The ablation shows policy breadth matters: going from MLCommons-only to MLCommons+OpenAI policy (CG-Full) increases JailJudge accuracy by +4.7% on the GPT-OSS-20B backbone, and a Static (No-RAG) baseline underperforms the RAG-grounded configurations on JailJudge/WildGuard/HarmBench despite matching or exceeding on AdvBenchM. The paper also reports a secondary, qualitative finding: manual review of CourtGuard's disagreements with ground-truth labels on JailJudge/HarmBench surfaced label noise -- two illustrative examples (Table 2) show items labeled SAFE in JailJudge that CourtGuard correctly flags UNSAFE against explicit MLCommons policy clauses.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is CourtGuard, a retrieval-augmented, model-agnostic multi-agent framework that reframes LLM safety classification as 'Evidentiary Debate': an Attacker agent and a Defender agent argue over a target LLM response, grounded via RAG (recursive-character-split chunks embedded with a sentence transformer, indexed in FAISS, top-k retrieval) in external governance documents (e.g., MLCommons Safety Policy, OpenAI Usage Policies, GDPR, Wikipedia's WP:VANDAL). A Judge agent then reads the N-round (default N=2) debate history and assigns a regulatory-threat score (1-3) and a practical-threat score (1-3); the summed rating (2-6) is deterministically mapped to SAFE (<=3), BORDERLINE (=4), or UNSAFE (>=5). Because safety logic lives in retrieved policy text rather than fine-tuned weights, the same architecture can be repointed at a new policy corpus at inference time with no retraining, and it is backbone-agnostic (validated with Llama-3-70B and GPT-OSS-20B).
threat_model: The paper does not model an attacker against CourtGuard itself; instead CourtGuard is the defense/detector under test. The 'threats' it evaluates are: (1) jailbreak/adversarial-prompt responses from a target LLM (attack methods drawn from prior work: GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP, DrAttack, DAN, Puzzler, an Andriushchenko-et-al. adaptive attack, plus Alpaca as a benign control), and (2) out-of-domain policy-violation content (Wikipedia vandalism edits, GDPR/InfoSoc privacy and IP violations) where the harm definition itself, not just the attack technique, is what varies. CourtGuard's own stated risk is that its Attacker/Defender debate agents could hallucinate false safety facts or default to the base model's generic parametric morality instead of the target policy -- which is exactly what the RAG citation-grounding is designed to mitigate, per the paper's framing in Sec. 2.2/3.
limitations: The paper self-reports exactly two limitations (Sec. 6): (1) an inference-vs-adaptation trade-off -- avoiding retraining costs by using retrieval + multi-turn debate instead incurs higher inference latency than a single-pass classifier; (2) instruction adherence -- smaller/less capable backbone models can fail to emit the required structured output, causing parsing errors that a simple binary classification head would not have. Additional caveats evident from the results themselves: on the Gold Standard suite the dedicated GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B baseline still beats CourtGuard on raw accuracy (0.919 vs 0.918/0.914), which the authors attribute to taxonomy alignment between that baseline's training and the evaluation's ground truth -- an acknowledgment that CourtGuard's edge is not universal. Static guardrails (e.g., LlamaGuard 4, JailJudge-Finetuned, WildGuard) remain competitive or superior to CourtGuard on several individual benchmark columns in Table 1 (e.g., WildGuard leads the macro average at 0.86/0.84, effectively tied with CourtGuard's best config). The zero-shot legal-domain evaluation (GDPR/InfoSoc) rests on only 33 cases reviewed by a single legal expert, and the Wikipedia vandalism transfer test uses N=100. The paper does not evaluate robustness of CourtGuard to adversarial manipulation of the debate process itself (e.g., prompt injection targeting the Attacker/Defender/Judge agents), nor does it report cost/latency numbers quantifying the trade-off it names in its limitations section.

datasets_used: AdvBenchM (custom, N=50, adaptive-attack responses per Andriushchenko et al. 2025, expert-verified 100% ASR)
HarmBench (N=210, 10 attack methods: GCG, AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP, DrAttack, DAN)
JailJudge (N=300, 14 hazard categories x 5 complexity levels)
PAN Wikipedia Vandalism Corpus 2010 (N=100 balanced subset, out-of-domain zero-shot test)

Human-Verified 'Gold Standard' Attack Suite (9 attack methodologies: DrAttack, Puzzler, AutoDAN, DAN, GCG, PAIR, TAP, Andriushchenko adaptive attack, Alpaca; 3 annotators, kappa=0.786, labeled against MLCommons Safety Policy)
33-case GDPR Art. 5/6 + InfoSoc Directive legal/privacy adaptation set (expert-reviewed)
quantitative_results: Macro average (8 benchmarks): CourtGuard-GPT-OSS-20B (2 Iter) 0.87 Acc / 0.86 F1 -- best overall
LlamaGuard 4 macro average: 0.77 Acc / 0.75 F1 (CourtGuard reported as +10% accuracy over this)
WildGuard baseline macro average: 0.86 Acc / 0.84 F1 (ties CourtGuard-Llama-70B 2 Iter)
Gold Standard suite: CourtGuard-GPT-OSS-20B (1 Iter) Recall 0.957, F1 0.904, F2 0.935, ROC AUC 0.924 (best)
Gold Standard suite: GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B Accuracy 0.919 (best raw accuracy, attributed to taxonomy match with ground truth)
ShieldGemma 9B Gold Standard F1: 0.056 (worst, cited as example of static-model failure)
Zero-shot Wikipedia Vandalism accuracy: CourtGuard 90%, gpt-oss-safeguard-20b 90%, base gpt-oss-20b 86%
GDPR/InfoSoc legal adaptation: Expert-AI Alignment rate 93.9% (N=33 cases)
Ablation: CG-MLC to CG-Full policy breadth increases JailJudge accuracy by +4.7% on GPT-OSS-20B backbone
Ablation: Static (No-RAG) baseline on GPT-OSS-20B scores 1.00/1.00 on AdvBenchM but only 0.817/0.794 on JailJudge, underperforming CG-Full (1 Iter) 0.893/0.893
